---
schema: verdi.deviation/v1
covers: 06b4e20e437f29e0983c606ecc7871b6d20b1c01
findings:
  - { id: judged-ac2-vacuous-on-real-tree, kind: judged, text: "AC-2 holds only vacuously against the repo it exists to protect. After DC-4 retired the sole skill, the only enumerated real instruction file is root CLAUDE.md, which names CLI verbs exclusively as bare backticked words (`gate`, `board`, `close`, `gc`, ...) with no `verdi ` prefix — a shape DC-1's extractor (verdiVerbInvocationRe inside backtick spans) is deliberately blind to. So on the real tree AC-2 validates zero references (zero-found, not checked-and-clean), and the exact drift class AC-2 names as its target ('a verb removed outright') would go undetected if e.g. `close` or `gc` were removed from dispatch.go, because the repo's own verb inventory sentence never enters extraction. The limitation is disclosed in a test comment (instructionverbs_test.go DC-1 note) but nowhere in the check's output, and AC-5's 'zero findings' green is indistinguishable from genuine verb-reference coverage. (confidence 0.55)", disposition: accepted-deviation, note: "DC-1 explicitly discloses this exact gap: AC-2 extraction targets only the verdi-plus-verb invocation shape inside backtick spans, never a bare backticked verb word with no prefix. CLAUDE.md names CLI verbs as bare backticked words, so it passes AC-2 vacuously today (zero found, not zero checked-clean) -- disclosed by DC-1, not hidden, with the reversible remedy named: widen the pattern later if a real drift case demonstrates the gap matters in practice. Accepted as designed, not a defect." }
  - { id: judged-ac3-disclosure-bare-substrings, kind: judged, text: "AC-3's disclosure set still contains the vacuous-satisfaction defect class the build itself discovered and only partially fixed. hasRitualDisclosure clears a finding if the bare substrings 'retired' or 'grandfathered' appear anywhere in the file, however unrelated to the ritual: a SKILL.md teaching `verdi board commit` as the current step passes silently if any other sentence happens to contain 'retired' (e.g. prose about a different retired verb, or even 'retiredness' via substring match). The implementers proved this class is real in this exact repo — unrelated sticky-disposition prose containing bare 'superseded' would have vacuously satisfied the check against the motivating defect file, which is why that one entry was narrowed to 'is superseded' (R4-I-72) — but 'retired' and 'grandfathered' were left bare with no anchoring ('is retired', word-boundary, or ritual-proximity), so the same suppression path remains open. The spec's disclosed lexical limit covers paraphrase evasion of the current-phrase set, not unrelated-text satisfaction of the disclosure set. (confidence 0.50)", disposition: accepted-deviation, note: "DC-3 deliberately defines the AC-3 disclosure check as bare-substring presence anywhere in the same file, not proximity-anchored, so the rewrite-to-disclose alternative stays satisfiable. R4-I-72 (ledger) already examined this exact false-clearing risk for retired and grandfathered -- the same class narrowed for superseded to is-superseded -- and left them bare after confirming neither collides in either real enumerated file. CO-2 discloses the whole tripwire as an imperfect lexical heuristic, with hardening explicitly deferred to a future judged-sweep-shaped story. Accepted as a disclosed, previously-examined residual, not a new defect." }
  - { id: judged-ac2-non-verb-token-false-positive, kind: judged, text: "verdiVerbInvocationRe captures any \\S+ following 'verdi' inside a backtick span, so tokens that are not verb references — quoting the CLI's own usage line as `verdi \u003cverb\u003e`, or writing `verdi --help` / `verdi -v` — are execed as the sole argument and, hitting the unknown-verb banner, fail a legitimate instruction file naming '\u003cverb\u003e' or '--help' as an unrecognized verb. AC-2's text scopes the check to 'command reference[s]' naming verbs; the extractor cannot distinguish a placeholder or flag from a verb, and the clean fixture (AC-4's no-false-positive proof) contains no such token, so this false-positive class is both unexercised and undisclosed. Latent today (no current instruction file quotes the usage shape) but it converts an honest doc edit — e.g. a skill quoting the usage banner — into a gate failure. (confidence 0.35)", disposition: accepted-deviation, note: "Verified live against the real binary: verdi --help, verdi \u003cverb\u003e, and verdi -v each print the identical top-level unknown-verb banner (dispatch.go special-cases no flag), confirming verdiVerbInvocationRe (which captures any non-whitespace token after verdi with no verb-shape filter) would false-positive if an enumerated file ever quoted the usage line or a flag inside backticks. Confirmed latent, not live: go test ./internal/specalign/... is green and neither real enumerated file (CLAUDE.md; .claude/skills/ is empty) contains such a token today, so AC-5 holds on the real tree. A disclosed extraction-precision residual in the same forward-looking spirit as DC-1/CO-2 (harden later if a real instance surfaces), not a violation of any AC as currently authored." }
digest: sha256:3855ee445b00579501c40577f204f6c22e6e399da2b65e13a8a7ac5d130b4dbe
integrity: sha256:f7cae347d02c3c2aed389c4dc65ade3ebf22149cef7cefaf0979116764d30215
judge_integrity: { stdin_b64: , raw_result: "{\"findings\":[{\"id\":\"ac2-vacuous-on-real-tree\",\"text\":\"AC-2 holds only vacuously against the repo it exists to protect. After DC-4 retired the sole skill, the only enumerated real instruction file is root CLAUDE.md, which names CLI verbs exclusively as bare backticked words (`gate`, `board`, `close`, `gc`, ...) with no `verdi ` prefix — a shape DC-1's extractor (verdiVerbInvocationRe inside backtick spans) is deliberately blind to. So on the real tree AC-2 validates zero references (zero-found, not checked-and-clean), and the exact drift class AC-2 names as its target ('a verb removed outright') would go undetected if e.g. `close` or `gc` were removed from dispatch.go, because the repo's own verb inventory sentence never enters extraction. The limitation is disclosed in a test comment (instructionverbs_test.go DC-1 note) but nowhere in the check's output, and AC-5's 'zero findings' green is indistinguishable from genuine verb-reference coverage.\",\"confidence\":0.55},{\"id\":\"ac3-disclosure-bare-substrings\",\"text\":\"AC-3's disclosure set still contains the vacuous-satisfaction defect class the build itself discovered and only partially fixed. hasRitualDisclosure clears a finding if the bare substrings 'retired' or 'grandfathered' appear anywhere in the file, however unrelated to the ritual: a SKILL.md teaching `verdi board commit` as the current step passes silently if any other sentence happens to contain 'retired' (e.g. prose about a different retired verb, or even 'retiredness' via substring match). The implementers proved this class is real in this exact repo — unrelated sticky-disposition prose containing bare 'superseded' would have vacuously satisfied the check against the motivating defect file, which is why that one entry was narrowed to 'is superseded' (R4-I-72) — but 'retired' and 'grandfathered' were left bare with no anchoring ('is retired', word-boundary, or ritual-proximity), so the same suppression path remains open. The spec's disclosed lexical limit covers paraphrase evasion of the current-phrase set, not unrelated-text satisfaction of the disclosure set.\",\"confidence\":0.5},{\"id\":\"ac2-non-verb-token-false-positive\",\"text\":\"verdiVerbInvocationRe captures any \\\\S+ following 'verdi' inside a backtick span, so tokens that are not verb references — quoting the CLI's own usage line as `verdi \u003cverb\u003e`, or writing `verdi --help` / `verdi -v` — are execed as the sole argument and, hitting the unknown-verb banner, fail a legitimate instruction file naming '\u003cverb\u003e' or '--help' as an unrecognized verb. AC-2's text scopes the check to 'command reference[s]' naming verbs; the extractor cannot distinguish a placeholder or flag from a verb, and the clean fixture (AC-4's no-false-positive proof) contains no such token, so this false-positive class is both unexercised and undisclosed. Latent today (no current instruction file quotes the usage shape) but it converts an honest doc edit — e.g. a skill quoting the usage banner — into a gate failure.\",\"confidence\":0.35}]}" }
provenance: { generator: verdi-align, version: v0, inputs: [spec/instruction-conformance@06b4e20e437f29e0983c606ecc7871b6d20b1c01, spec/instruction-conformance@38fd824509298f49fd5f393cd083d3841ab69fe0], digest: sha256:3855ee445b00579501c40577f204f6c22e6e399da2b65e13a8a7ac5d130b4dbe, integrity: sha256:f7cae347d02c3c2aed389c4dc65ade3ebf22149cef7cefaf0979116764d30215, model: sha256:a8b2e3c1eb4defba461355077a2b82ae13db212beaad6e78eab05db51e5b6721 }
---
# Alignment report

## Computed

(none)

### Boundary diff vs acceptance baseline

(no impacted services)

### Diagram alignment

- (no accepted proposals)
- (no illustrative diagrams in this spec's body)

## Judged

- **judged-ac2-vacuous-on-real-tree** [accepted-deviation]: AC-2 holds only vacuously against the repo it exists to protect. After DC-4 retired the sole skill, the only enumerated real instruction file is root CLAUDE.md, which names CLI verbs exclusively as bare backticked words (`gate`, `board`, `close`, `gc`, ...) with no `verdi ` prefix — a shape DC-1's extractor (verdiVerbInvocationRe inside backtick spans) is deliberately blind to. So on the real tree AC-2 validates zero references (zero-found, not checked-and-clean), and the exact drift class AC-2 names as its target ('a verb removed outright') would go undetected if e.g. `close` or `gc` were removed from dispatch.go, because the repo's own verb inventory sentence never enters extraction. The limitation is disclosed in a test comment (instructionverbs_test.go DC-1 note) but nowhere in the check's output, and AC-5's 'zero findings' green is indistinguishable from genuine verb-reference coverage. (confidence 0.55) — DC-1 explicitly discloses this exact gap: AC-2 extraction targets only the verdi-plus-verb invocation shape inside backtick spans, never a bare backticked verb word with no prefix. CLAUDE.md names CLI verbs as bare backticked words, so it passes AC-2 vacuously today (zero found, not zero checked-clean) -- disclosed by DC-1, not hidden, with the reversible remedy named: widen the pattern later if a real drift case demonstrates the gap matters in practice. Accepted as designed, not a defect.
- **judged-ac3-disclosure-bare-substrings** [accepted-deviation]: AC-3's disclosure set still contains the vacuous-satisfaction defect class the build itself discovered and only partially fixed. hasRitualDisclosure clears a finding if the bare substrings 'retired' or 'grandfathered' appear anywhere in the file, however unrelated to the ritual: a SKILL.md teaching `verdi board commit` as the current step passes silently if any other sentence happens to contain 'retired' (e.g. prose about a different retired verb, or even 'retiredness' via substring match). The implementers proved this class is real in this exact repo — unrelated sticky-disposition prose containing bare 'superseded' would have vacuously satisfied the check against the motivating defect file, which is why that one entry was narrowed to 'is superseded' (R4-I-72) — but 'retired' and 'grandfathered' were left bare with no anchoring ('is retired', word-boundary, or ritual-proximity), so the same suppression path remains open. The spec's disclosed lexical limit covers paraphrase evasion of the current-phrase set, not unrelated-text satisfaction of the disclosure set. (confidence 0.50) — DC-3 deliberately defines the AC-3 disclosure check as bare-substring presence anywhere in the same file, not proximity-anchored, so the rewrite-to-disclose alternative stays satisfiable. R4-I-72 (ledger) already examined this exact false-clearing risk for retired and grandfathered -- the same class narrowed for superseded to is-superseded -- and left them bare after confirming neither collides in either real enumerated file. CO-2 discloses the whole tripwire as an imperfect lexical heuristic, with hardening explicitly deferred to a future judged-sweep-shaped story. Accepted as a disclosed, previously-examined residual, not a new defect.
- **judged-ac2-non-verb-token-false-positive** [accepted-deviation]: verdiVerbInvocationRe captures any \S+ following 'verdi' inside a backtick span, so tokens that are not verb references — quoting the CLI's own usage line as `verdi <verb>`, or writing `verdi --help` / `verdi -v` — are execed as the sole argument and, hitting the unknown-verb banner, fail a legitimate instruction file naming '<verb>' or '--help' as an unrecognized verb. AC-2's text scopes the check to 'command reference[s]' naming verbs; the extractor cannot distinguish a placeholder or flag from a verb, and the clean fixture (AC-4's no-false-positive proof) contains no such token, so this false-positive class is both unexercised and undisclosed. Latent today (no current instruction file quotes the usage shape) but it converts an honest doc edit — e.g. a skill quoting the usage banner — into a gate failure. (confidence 0.35) — Verified live against the real binary: verdi --help, verdi <verb>, and verdi -v each print the identical top-level unknown-verb banner (dispatch.go special-cases no flag), confirming verdiVerbInvocationRe (which captures any non-whitespace token after verdi with no verb-shape filter) would false-positive if an enumerated file ever quoted the usage line or a flag inside backticks. Confirmed latent, not live: go test ./internal/specalign/... is green and neither real enumerated file (CLAUDE.md; .claude/skills/ is empty) contains such a token today, so AC-5 holds on the real tree. A disclosed extraction-precision residual in the same forward-looking spirit as DC-1/CO-2 (harden later if a real instance surfaces), not a violation of any AC as currently authored.
